author: @CitizenJourno-1
text: We had a water system using a small Creek where we captured the water in a pipe and put it into a 50 gallon barrel with a lid and out the other side a pipe came out from the top third of the barrel downstream. The reason was any debris that came in the intake pipe would settle in the barrel and clean water would come out the pipe. If you made that a four to five foot pipe with some support you could have the equivalent of a hose hitting your turbine out of even the smallest stream without ruining any of the topography. Basically you're increasing your head pressure or drop by extending that pipe horizontally over the hillside. No moving parts no debris just clean water dumping over your turbine

author: @endeshaw1000
text:
Hi Rob. Im still not totally finished with the super-condensator idea, but meanwhile am working on storing energy in form of pressured air. The main problem is still the decharging rate and self destruction if electrolyte dries out. So I organized a 12V compressor with a little storage tank and print (3d) self developed air turbines as well as trying out bought ones. I wonder if you could pimp the 	efficiency by creating a water circle that is driving the rotor. 

Imagen this: A closed system (not air tight / with exhaust - will understand later)(box of plexi glass with cable ( +/ -) in and out)in the lower 1/3 is water(or oil cause oil doesnt vaporize so easy) that can be pumped into a container above (flow only one direction - ensured with valve). In addition you have a 12V air comressor within that box with little storage container that is connected through an electric useable valve, to the container above. Also only one direction possible through valve. When you then use a bit of solar energy to fill the upper container with liquid from below (Waterpump), then use the solar energy to set the little air tank under preassure, and then open electrically the valve so the preasure in the above container pushes the water with force out of the container, through a turbine (electricity generated can leave the system) and then back to the bottom of the box - couldnt this way the turbine use the air pressure created much more efficient? liquid recollects in lower 1/3 , air pressure can leave through exhaust, and force is hopefully used much more efficient.  Could you please try out for us? :)

author: @shinjisan2015
text: Two of these would be good as additional trickle chargers for home battery and solar setups. Have one as a wind turbine and the other as a water turbine with two water tanks. One water tank on the roof and one on the ground. During the day a solar panel runs a water pump to pump from the ground tank to the roof tank. At night once the house battery is depleted to a certain level a valve opens on the roof tank and slowly the water drains into the ground tank passing over one of these turbines as a jet first. Extends the charge of the battery overnight. Of course you'd need anti-freeze for sub-zero climates, but why not?

author: @KeithOlson
text:
Neat!  One thing that really caught my attention was how bulbous the leading edge was at 3:00.  It puzzled me for a second, but then I realized how genius that it is.  If you take a look at RMS's version, the leading edges were buckled to some extent because the material simply wasn't stiff enough.  Well, if you continue the material around into a long teardrop shape that follows the pattern in the patent, it will increase the robustness of the leading edge *dramatically,* especially if you fill the hollow with something stiff and lightweight, like foam.  (Essentially, use the same materials and techniques that are used to create lightweight aircraft wings.)

Cheers!

author: @khug
text:
I think the whole point of the shape of this turbine is that it was optimized assuming a consistent flow over the whole surface (e.g. in a current). It will certainly work with a jet pointed at one side, but that's not what makes this turbine special. 

Excited to see the river tests! Lots of tidal currents in the UK :-)

author: @simongross3122
text:
Yes, this is fascinating, thank you.  

Perhaps this is the ideal shape for a water-wheel which were once in common use for cutting lumber or grinding flour.  I wonder if it is also a good design for a paddle-wheel boat.  These have always seemed to have flat paddles.  I wonder if the curved shape would have placed too much strain on the mechanism when the only material available was wood.

I am excited by the idea of it completely submerged in ocean currents.  Of course in that scenario there would probably not be so much flywheel effect.  But I think there are lots of places in the world that have very strong underwater currents which could be used.

replies.author: @strictnonconformist7369
replies.text:
I think the problem with having it fully submerged in ocean currents is that you’d have the issue of sealing off the central axis cause problems for maintenance.

I’m guessing it’d make more sense to make them of a large enough diameter to get the central axis out of the water.

The most interesting thing about this for sea water usage is the natural shape appears to not be as likely to get biofouled, but that clearly requires testing, as well as finding out how it performs in near-shore waves and receding tides.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes I am quiet excited by that idea too - cheers

replies.author: @simongross3122
replies.text: @strictnonconformist7369 Those are good points.  I think I did see a video once about underwater turbines, but I don't think they were this shape, and I don't remember how they solved those problems.

author: @TetianaBulgakova
text: A few words: The Ugrinsky turbine is indeed a water turbine. This was published in 1950. But as a wind turbine, it was published in 1961 in the journal "Техника молодежи" No. 4 (p42) with some modifications and other authorship. But the geometry is still the same. I used Ugninsky's original geometry in my wind turbine.

@ThinkingandTinkering no sir, im just another poor african in africa.
 i cant sleep sometimes so i design different energy systems running simulations for different designs in my head until I pass out.
having no tools and no tech and only getting electrickery for 4 to 5hrs per day.
bit of a challenge.
its taken 4 months to gather 2 broken microwaves.
soon i will build an ammeter and voltmeter to test the circuits.
eventually will build "free" energy system and give it away to my people.
so that they never have to suffer without power any more

replies.author: @esahg5421
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering conch shells have those ridges to create cavitation and increase rotation when thrown underwater.

i would throw conch shells under water and saw that if they didn't have those ridges then in would tumble.
originally trying to find who is blowing the bubbles, i know now that that is cavitation bubbles.

picture a whirlpool in the center a conch shell suspended. that shell would try to resist the flow, creating cavitation then boosting it around to match the whirlpool flow.

basically a conch is resistant to initial flow, but once it cavitates it really gets going.

author: @mitchellsmith7868
text: What about a car electric motor. It produces 737 lb-ft of torque at any where in between 500 and 2800 Rpm. Hooked up with two of those expensive batteries the way it can be switched in between batteries. Hook the drive shaft up to a crankshaft and drive 8 linear generators. With the right cooling apparatus. You should produce enough power to charge one pack of the batteries, and enough to power what ever else was wanted.

replies.author: @you-know-who.
replies.text: Conservation of energy just left the chat. I like how you think... Reminds me of me ten years ago

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate but are you suggesting we strap motor to a generator? with the wrinkle of a couple of batteries in between?

replies.author: @mitchellsmith7868
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yeah as long as the energy is coming from a storage device, and one of those electric car motors can produce alot of torque 737 ft-lbs up to 2800 rpms. With the fact that the energy coming from the battery and is converted to 220v at a suggested amount of amps, and then you have two batteries the way the battery is charged not under a full load with the motor hooked up on a crankshaft you could push said amount of linear generators be it v4, v6, or V8 it is all just the amount of improvement you can make on preexisting technologies that propels us into the future. I have actually been trying to figure out a way that to store the energy generated and use it to push the device in said question first I started with thinking about air compression look at some of Jeremiah's videos from the last two years, and look at his comment feeds also did alot of talking behind the scenes with testurenergy not that he used any thing that I came up with, but putting a tankless hot water heater on his unit. They also have a a design that had my whole theory in a working device that is called vacudest. The only difference is they would have to add a 1/4 line after the compression stage to the tesla turbine that turbine is not supposed to run over 9000 rpm for 218 horsepower I think that is the numbers, but good luck telling testurenergy that because their design is nothing like the design of tesla I forgot the size of the turbine I think that it is 9in though the one in question. It was in millimeters had to convert it, but yeah that is my idea. I have sacrificed my own dreams and desires to try, and figure this out. Also forgot how I put it in those comments about the energy storage device.

author: @angrydoggy9170
text: I tried to calculate the energy produced by the water running of my roof in a serious downpour. That made me wonder about placing small turbines.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there can be quite a lot of water

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: zero is a bit harsh - there will be power there but as you know it will depend on lots of factors - how big is the roof, how much rainfall, how far the drop etc etc

replies.author: @angrydoggy9170
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering It’s quite a big roof 35-16 meters and we’ve got quite a bit of rainfall, drop is 4 meters. I just don’t think the cost for the equipment and maintenance would surpass the energy cost.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @angrydoggy9170 yeah that's quite possibly true - however it would be interesting in itself and if you made and scavenged a whole lot the cost needn't be too high - Personally I think it would be fascinating just to try it and see - I don't think it will solve the energy crisis but I do think a whole lot can be learnt and if that was the main objective it would have a lot of value in and of itself - it's really why I thought sabret00th's comment was a bit naff - if you think about it rainfall collection is all hydrogenation is - true it's on large scale but it is also what Canada uses as its main power source lol

replies.author: @angrydoggy9170
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I did try it with a toy waterwheel, but the thing fell apart pretty soon. :)

author: @Liowen
text: I believe submerging it would reduce the torque somewhat due to the ends being pushed on by weight of the water, basically acting like a friction material.  I would think that it acting more like a water wheel would be the best bet as it would disrupt the surface tension allowing it to spin faster.  But then you still have the issue of water proofing the motor so it doesn't get all gunked up, maybe a "gear" (pulley and belt type) driven motor to keep the motor out of the water would be the easiest solution to that issue.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: maybe - but if you think about it one way air is just a fluid and a river is pretty analogous to  an airflow
